title: C Dynamic Memory
content:
### Dynamic Memory in C

Dynamic memory allocation allows you to allocate memory at runtime from the heap.

### Heap vs Stack

| Feature | Stack | Heap |
|---------|-------|------|
| Allocation | Automatic | Manual (`malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`) |
| Deallocation | Automatic (scope exit) | Manual (`free()`) |
| Size limit | Small (KB-MB) | Large (available RAM) |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Lifetime | Function scope | Until freed |
| Fragmentation | None | Possible |

### Key Functions (from `<stdlib.h>`)

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `malloc()` | Allocates uninitialized memory |
| `calloc()` | Allocates zero-initialized memory |
| `realloc()` | Resizes previously allocated memory |
| `free()` | Releases allocated memory |

### Common Mistakes

- Forgetting to `free()` (memory leak)
- Using memory after `free()` (dangling pointer)
- Double `free()` (undefined behavior)
- Not checking `NULL` return from allocation

---

> **Key Point:** Every `malloc()`/`calloc()`/`realloc()` must be matched with a `free()`. Always check the return value for NULL.

quiz.question: What happens if you forget to free dynamically allocated memory?
